title: Stable Diffusion vs Midjourney vs DALL-E 3: Testing Limits in the AI Art Prompt Battle!
description:
Welcome to a fascinating exploration of AI-generated art! In this video, I'm putting three major AI art platforms - Stable Diffusion, Midjourney, and DALL-E 3 - to the test. We're going to see how these AIs handle a variety of art styles and prompts, using a cute bunny portrait as our subject. Whether you're an art enthusiast or simply curious about AI capabilities, this video offers a unique glimpse into the world of digital creativity.

🎨 Experiment Overview:

- See how Stable Diffusion's Realism Engine SDXL v3, Midjourney v6, and DALL-E 3 each interpret a bunny portrait.
- Be amazed by the combination of different art styles, like cave painting with Sci-Fi and Illuminated Manuscript with Biopunk, and how each AI platform handles these creative challenges.

👀 Key Highlights:

- Watch as I mix and match styles like Neo-Romanticism with Cybergoth and Art Deco with Cyberpunk, observing the unique results from each AI.
- Discover which AI platform excels in areas like photorealism, logo
transcript: Today I am conducting two experiments. I am testing some prompts in the most popular AI-generated  platforms. Stable diffusion, mid-journey, and dally-three. At the same time, I am combining different  art styles to achieve a unique look. We will see how well they understand the art styles and what  images they produce. I will conduct the experiments using a portrait of a cute bunny to observe how  each AI interprets it. For stable diffusion, I am utilizing the realism engine SDXL version three.  In the case of mid-journey, I am employing version six. Lastly, with chat GPT-4, I am utilizing  dally-three. We'll start by testing with just one style like a cave painting. Dally did a good  job at capturing that style accurately. Next, we'll switch gears and try something completely  different like a sci-fi art style. All platforms did well with this style, but what's interesting  is when we combine two styles like cave painting and sci-fi. It creates something entirely new,  blending elements from both worlds and giving us unique images. The next test, I combined  illuminated manuscript art with biopunk art style. Stable diffusion consistently provided good  results each time. Mid-journey and dally required a few generations to get close to what I was  expecting. For the naïve art and techware-fashioned style, stable diffusion consistently provided me  with reliable results. However, the other platforms only included techware in half of the generations.  Here's an interesting combination of neo-romanticism, art, and cybergoth art style. I anticipated  something darker from dally, but it seems to prefer more cheerful and colorful imagery. When it comes  to mannerism art and solar punk art style, only stable diffusion appears to incorporate solar punk  elements consistently. For the combination of art deco and cyberpunk art style, stable diffusion  also provided me with pleasing results. Here's an interesting mix. In nominee pot trees.  Except for dally, I'm not quite sure what kind of artistic blend was attempted there.  And here's one that produced an explosion of colors because it's combined with the psychedelic style.  Another mix where dally seems to take things to literally? I mean, I asked for cute, but somehow it  went overboard with both cuteness and color, stable diffusion and mid-journey on the other hand  produce similar and nice results. Let's see how constructivism and emo-fashion art style work  together. Here dally captured the emo mood better. For art brute and sci-fi art style, I obtained  some similar results, except for mid-journey, which varied significantly in each generation.  It leaned more towards a rough graffiti style. For this mix of styles, each one yields different  results. What I've noticed is that if the ratio isn't square, sometimes I get those empty spaces  on both portrait and landscape ratios for dally. For hard-edge painting and necropunk art style,  dally seems to avoid the dark, gritty aesthetic of the painting and leans towards something  lighter instead. While it's proficient at illustrations, it appears to try to render  everything into an illustrative style. For ancient Egyptian mural and vaporwave art style,  each AI has its own interpretation of the style. Same goes for this mix of cloisonism art and  pixel art style. So I suggest playing around with different style combinations on your favorite AI  and see what interesting results you can get. And once more, each AI offers a unique  interpretation of Tarot de Marseille art and Honeywell art style. Here's a cool style I achieved  by mixing Mayan art with neon lighting portrait art style. Sometimes blending opposite art styles  produces the most intriguing results. For this mix, I obtained completely different results. So  when selecting which AI to use beyond considering the price, you're choosing based on the type of  images and style you want to produce. When it comes to vector designs or designs that can be  easily vectorized, Dully typically delivers the best results for icons, logos, and simple  vector style illustrations. For a text, I find that the most accurate results come from Dully,  mid-journey is not far behind, and with enough attempts, you can get one or two words.  Stable diffusion, however, tends to make more mistakes. While it can generate short popular words,  it struggles with more specific text. However, I've noticed that stable cascade, which was recently  released for testing, seems to generate better text. For a complex prompt understanding, Dully  is definitely the best. Mid-journey also does well, capturing some aspects accurately while struggling  with others. Stable diffusion, on the other hand, seems to be confused when too many colors are  involved and often struggles to depict specific elements such as a green hat. When it comes to  photography and realism, stable diffusion and mid-journey excel, producing great results, however,  Dully seems to struggle significantly in achieving a realistic look. For logo design, Dully typically  outperforms the rest, it has a knack for achieving that simple yet interesting look that's often  desired in a logo. When it comes to coloring pages, mid-journey tends to struggle more than the rest  in achieving the desired look. And if it's cuteness you're after, Dully reigns supreme in delivering  adorable results. Here's a mix of dark gothic and fantasy digital painting. Each AI approach the  style differently. When it comes to horror comics mixes, mid-journey tends to emphasize the horror  aspect more while the others focus more on the comic style. However, of all three, Dully is the  most strict. It declined some of my requests, particularly if the prompt involves superpowers and  a very dark style. Here's a cool mix to try. A cinematic photo with an epic sci-fi robotic style.  I always enjoy seeing the results of these types of generations. Here's a delicious prompt  of a bunny made from chocolate. Yummy. I got some different results on each AI, but stable diffusion  wasn't able to add the eyepatch as I wanted. I got a few generations with a patch on both eyes,  but not a pirate one. Here's how I see things. It doesn't mean I'm right or wrong. It's just my  perspective. Ultimately, you have to decide what's important to you and which AI best suits your needs.  Stable diffusion is open source, so it's free if you install it on your computer. However,  you'll need a good computer with a quality video card and it works best with Nvidia. There are  alternatives online, but they are paid services. So I'm comparing here with the installed version  of stable diffusion on your computer. For mid-journey, prices range from $10 to $120.  However, if you want unlimited generation, you'll need to opt for the $30 version or higher.  As for Dolly, it's $20 per month, and you have access to chat GPT as well. The only limitation is  that you're restricted to 30 messages with Dolly in three hours and you'll have to wait to  generate more. Stable diffusion works only on your computer, whereas the others work on most devices  since they are online platforms. All platforms can produce a good variety of styles, but Dolly  seems to struggle with achieving a photo-realistic look. For prompt understanding, Dolly is the best  followed by mid-journey and then stable diffusion. Therefore, you may need to refine your prompts to  achieve the desired results with stable diffusion. Each AI has its strengths and weaknesses.  For photo-realistic results, stable diffusion and mid-journey are the go-to options.  If you prefer illustrations or cartoon styles, all platforms are suitable. But Dolly excels in  this area. Additionally, for vector art styles, icons, and logos, Dolly is the better choice.  If you're looking for a strong artistic look, mid-journey tends to add a more artistic touch,  and if you prefer more control over the process, then stable diffusion is the way to go.  Stable diffusion requires more effort to use compared to the rest. It has a lot of capabilities,  but you need to learn how to utilize them effectively.  Mid-journey is somewhat easier to use with a few options available on Discord,  and the website makes it even more user-friendly. Dolly is the easiest to use. You simply communicate  with it in natural language, like explaining to an artist what you want. Stable diffusion is not  censored, so you can generate anything you want depending on the model you choose. Mid-journey  is censored to some extent, but Dolly is even more restricted. Dolly censors anything that  seems suspicious and refuses to generate content that may break guidelines, including celebrities,  copyrighted materials, dark styles, or themes involving superpowers. For those interested in NSFW  content, only stable diffusion can accommodate such requests. When it comes to control,  stable diffusion offers the most options. You have access to image-to-image,  control-net, in-painting, out-painting, various extensions, models, and Laura. Mid-journey also  provides some control with style, reference, and options, like very region-in-painting and out-painting.  However, Dolly doesn't offer as much control. You communicate your request to it,  and hope it can generate what you want, without manual tools for fine-tuning.  Stable diffusion offers a wide range of downloadable models that specialize in different styles or  subjects. Mid-journey provides both older and newer versions, typically labeled as normal, raw,  and knee-jee, with fewer options to choose from. Dolly, on the other hand, doesn't offer  their option to choose a model directly. Instead, you can create your own GPT model specialized  for specific tasks by providing instructions, allowing for customizations such as painting,  characters, vectors, and more. Among all AI, Dolly handles text the best. I can often add text to  images with fewer mistakes compared to other platforms. While it's not perfect, it's definitely  making progress in this area. With stable diffusion, you have the option to do batch generation,  or let it generate indefinitely. Mid-journey generates four images at once,  whereas Dolly generates only one image at a time. Among all three AI's, Dolly has the  fewest errors, particularly with hands, fingers, and how it handles objects. While it still has  some errors, they are noticeably fewer compared to the other platforms. Since the AI was trained  with small images, there is a limit on how big an image can be generated. Therefore, most platforms  generate images at 24 pixels, and then use an upscaler to enlarge them. With stable diffusion,  you can download and use a few upscaler models. Mid-journey offers two options. A subtle upscaler  that keeps things similar, and a creative upscaler that slightly modifies the result. However, Dolly  doesn't have any upscaler built in, so you need to use your own. Stable diffusion is the only AI  that allows you to train your own models using your own images and styles. This feature enables you  to create a model specifically tailored to the subject or style you desire. When it comes to privacy,  only stable diffusion offers full privacy since it operates on your own computer, allowing you to  control your data. In contrast, other platforms operate online, which means that the platform owners  or administrators may have access to the prompts and generated content. Images generated with stable  diffusion are private on your computer while for mid-journey, they are public unless you opt for  the $60 version and enable stealth mode. As for Dolly, it's likely that only the platform  administrator can view your generations, ensuring a level of privacy for your generated content.  Which AI is your favorite? Comment below. Also, I need a small favor. I've been trying to monetize  this channel for over a year, but I still need 600 watch hours, so something as simple as a share  or a like can help me a lot. Thank you for watching, and I'll see you soon with a new tutorial.
